speaker: interviewer
text: [DIGITAL TONE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Should we play with my glasses?

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you like to take a microphone too? Give me five seconds. And any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 15, 1995. Interview with Mrs. Gizel Herskovits Berman. Interviewer Janice Englehart, in Mercer Island, Washington. This interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you can just go sit down and say the same thing again. And we'll just roll through the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not permitted to make comments?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet. My name is Janice Englehart. Today is September 15, 1995. And I'm with Mrs. Gizel Berman in Mercer Island, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all yours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon, Mrs. Berman. Can you please state your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gizel Berman-- I don't have a middle name, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herskovits.

speaker: interviewer
text: The day in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 26, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the town in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sobrance, which is-- it was in Slovakia. It still is in Slovakia. Then, it was called Czechoslovakia. Now, it's only Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something about your early days in Sobrance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't realize while I was there how good I had it. It took me years later, that it was a very wonderful, charming, easy life. I lived there only until age 11, and then my parents wanted me to have higher education. So after the elementary-- grade schools-- yes-- I went to live in Uzhorod. But while I was in Sobrance, it was just delightfully pleasant, easy. And it-- it was a-- Czechoslovakia itself was a very strong Catholic country. There were not too many Jewish people in the community, but just enough that there were some. But I remember, in going to school, I figured out that maybe I'm better off if I go with a Catholic girl, one of my Catholic friends, because she always stopped by in the church and prayed. And I knew she didn't study much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided I will try to do the same thing. I will go with her. And I didn't do any big performance over the praying, but I just asked the Lord to please help me make it good. I-- I too didn't study very much. So it was fun. It was-- it was really fun, and the countryside was beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very well-known spa real close by, about 3 kilometers. And it had a sulfur water, which wasn't very pleasant. But it was very well-known and much in demand. And people came from all over, there. So we had all these guests. And we always had a carriage, but we didn't have the horse. So if somebody came special, then we would rent a horse and somebody who would ride the carriage with the horse. And we had a cow by the name of Rose, because my mother believed in it that it's very important. The milk should be really of the best quality for the children. So we had our own cow. And consequently, when it was Jewish holidays, we would go into the city of Uzhorod, which was about 14 kilometers or 10 kilometers-- I'm not to good with this, to remember. Anyway, the cow came with us along the road, and behind the carriage. And when we got there, of course, they had to-- we made arrangements for the cow. But she was very unhappy and was making lots of noise. And my grandfather, where we-- with whom we spent the holidays, complained quite a bit that, if this cow cannot be more or better adjusted, she shouldn't come into the city. She should just stay out in the countryside, in Sobrance. But my mother insisted. We always had the cow with us, and the holidays were very pleasant. And the school was also very pleasant. Everybody knew everyone. It was just over a thousand people. And I will tell you highlights. For example, my mother insisted I should learn the piano from an early age. And so, it was the principal's son who was the piano teacher, and he taught me for six years. And after six years, he walked up to my mother and says, madam, I don't think I want to take your money any longer. Your daughter is a-- a hopeless case. So that was my piano learning experience. But otherwise, it was a very happy life, except, again, my mother was so particular how the daughters will be raised. I don't think they made all this fuss with my two older brothers. But she-- she really went all out the way that it should be special.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from day one, I guess, after I was born or whenever-- really early-- they get me a governess from the western part of Czechoslovakia, and that I will speak only German, because that was the language of culture. She wanted to me-- to grow up like a cultured young woman. And consequently, I didn't learn the Hungarian language, nor the Slovak language, only after age 6.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I started public school. Then my parents spoke to me, from that time on, Hungarian, and in the school, Slovak. So by the time of I was 7, I spoke all three languages fluently. So that was something my mother was very, very much concerned about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were lots of things. I could go on forever about my mother, because she was really very unusual. And we would put away, in those days-- I mean, like, 65 years ago-- vegetable for the winter in sand, and-- and cover up, in the-- in the big-- uh, what did we-- what do they-- we call it? Underground, a-- Cellar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. What is the underground house? A-- not a cave. We had even ice, we put in there. Oh, there's a name for it. There is a definite English-- anyway, that's where we kept the vegetables. And in order to go down to this lower level of the house, we had to have a tremendous-- like a 15-inch-long key, and to use that. But it was very important, because we bought the apples, and pears, and-- and carrots, and cabbage. It-- it was quite a production. But my mother took real pride that we would eat fresh vegetab-- cellar, thank you. That's the word, cellar. [LAUGHS] So we didn't have wine in the cellar, but we had lots of vegetable. And so childhood was quite, quite easy and not easy, the same time, because my mother was very, very particular and demanding. And every time I came back from school, she would make me report either to her or to the governess. And they would look into my hair, because it was quite frequ-- quite often that I would come home from school, and I would have lice in my hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Because there were children who lived under different circumstances. And I-- we would hug. I'd [INAUDIBLE] their clothes, and I would be full of lice. So then, that was always quite a procedure, and sometimes it happened three, four times a week. And I was de-liced. What about your father? Can you tell us something about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a very easygoing, fine gentleman-- very, very fine, especially to me. I think he never spanked me, if I remember correctly. My mother was, though, the strict one with her words and with her looks. She would look at me, and I would j-- just shake in my shoes, that-- she really meant business. Don't move. Do this right. Do that right. Don't do that. But the father was very much liked in the community. He owned a bank. There were two banks in our little community, and one of them, he owned. And he was known for his reputation from way, way back that if somebody wanted something real badly, you just go to Mr. Herskovits. And you just tell your sad story, and he will be very understanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember lots of times that people would first come to the house and ask, could I come to the bank for official visit? But I need some special understanding. Um, so much understanding, when a little Gypsy group came to settle down in our community, and he-- they came to my father too. Could we have, someplace, some land?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just so happens that we did own several acres of land around the co-- village. And my father easily agreed that they give them a couple acres. But they have to behave, and-- and all kinds of restrictions, and no stealing, and things like that. And they promised everything. And the next thing we knew, yeah, they came [? first ?] [INAUDIBLE] a few times, over the weekend, and were serenading us, because they were very good musicians that played, very nicely, the violin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we said, that's lovely. Thank you very much, but you don't have to come that often. We shall let you know when we want it, OK? Fine. But then, in no time, two of them show up. And they're carrying a huge animal in their arm. It was a chicken, but it was a hen. It was a biggie one. And so my mother opens the door and sees them. And he says, Mrs. Herskovits, we want to show our appreciation, so we brought you this big chicken. And she says, look, I have a feeling you stole this chicken. But missus, we stole the biggest one. We tried to be really nice. She says, look, I understand it. We don't want to have anything to do with these things. Please do not come back. Please stop stealing. And I don't want small, or big, or anything. Just try to live a de-- as decently as you can, but don't-- not with stealing, all right? So they went away. We never saw them again, with any gifts.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was a nice feeling. If I think back, and when I really realized-- more so in this country-- there are minorities, and there are second class, and third class, and-- I wasn't aware at home. I really wasn't. Of course, as I said, I was among the lucky one, and I had everything. But I wasn't so aware until later. And then all those things, what I saw growing up, I carried it with me. So I never-- I never knew that feeling to look down at somebody, and it helped. It helped to enrich my life this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: One more [LAUGHS] unusual, uh, encounter that my mother always would threaten me. If I would-- I'm sure you heard about it, that in case I will do something really bad, she doesn't want to spank me. She just can't see, why should a girl be spanked? But she will find the proper punishment. So then, I found out the proper punishment would be that we had a chicken coop.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some chickens. And the chicken coop was so big that there could be chickens on the lower part, and the upper part was empty. And so there was always room-- would be always room to punish me or send me in. So I started to worry about it, because I-- I like to eat. And I-- anyway, I had my very definite likes and dislikes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I st-- had kind of prepared, in a-- um, in a serviette napkin-- some sweets, in case I have to go on my exile trip. I could just pick it up and go there. Sure enough, one day my mother found a good reason. And she said, now, you go and spend time in the chicken coop. And I remember, I was so proud of myself that I'm so prepared. And I grabbed my little package-- wherever it was hidden, I don't know anymore-- and went in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in no-- oh, I wasn't in, like, three, four minutes. And I thought that's un-- unbelievable! I don't think my mother knows what she's doing to me. This is painful. Those chicken lice would just be all over me, and I was itching like crazy. Well, they still left me there. I still had a governess, of course-- the same time [INAUDIBLE]. And sh-- they left me there for, like, 20 minutes, something like that. I may-- it seemed like very long. And then, they called me. My child, come out. You may come out, Gizel. And I didn't hurry out. I wanted to show how, no big deal. I can stay there. And then my mother got more upset, with her voice, and louder. You know, you better come out! I'm telling-- OK, I came out. And she said, now, you go right there, and point at a certain place. And stand there, and take off your clothes. And all-- you can keep the slip.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did it, and they said-- they realized I was just covered with lice, my whole body and hair, again, and everything. And they brought benzene. And benzene, that's gasoline. We-- we-- we didn't use it for the car. We didn't have a car, but I guess for-- we had it in the house. I don't know what else did they use it, besides on my hair, with the-- to de-- de-lice me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so I got really, like, a [INAUDIBLE] with this gasoline, benzene. I smelled like a horrible little monster. And I knew that I will not go back again to this chicken coop, that's for sure. And my mother, afterwards, gave me a big lecture. And that was unnecessary, and I should know better. I said, well, Mother, you always promised me. So I expected that, one day, you will send me there. So I had it. Things like that. Life-- this is the light side. There was nothing on the difficult side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name a few things-- one or two-- um, that you learned from your parents, that would serve you later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god! My whole life, I'm living all based on my mother's saying that-- uh, she had all those sayings, you know. In the kitchen, for example, there was a big embroidery, and I had to make it. I don't know. I was eight years old, nine years old, something like that-- with cross-stitches. And by the way, I started to do handy-work from way, way back, because I realize I'm handy, and I crave to use my hands. And here-- I'll go back on that subject. So no time, I realized there is a printed piece of fabric with blue printout for cross-stitches, and some flowers. And underneath, there was a big sign. It was written in German. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Love goes through the stomach. And I never forgot it, as you can see. And my mother made it very, very clear that if I just look up, that is a fact of life-- that the stomach has to be satisfied and con-- you know, treated well. OK, Mother, so I learned that one real fast. Then when I was sitting at the table, I had to sit very-- not just me, but my two brothers too-- we had to behave and sit just so, because if no, we might be sent away from the table. And I found out a wonderful gimmick. I would take out one of the soft breads from the table, put it in my lap. I had a napkin there. I sat beautifully straight, looking up. And meantime, with my two hands, I was creating little animals, birds mainly, or cows, and flowers. And each one was around, like, one inch long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would put them on the toothpick, right next to my place setting. And whoever sat next to me had a chance to make a comment. And usually, they was very much admired and appreciated, and I felt good. And so then, I would look at my mother and say, could I go, please, to the kitchen and bake a-- the lady next to me-- I don't know who they were, but probably they were friends of the family or relatives-- that they like it? So my mother, all right, child. You can go to the kitchen. I would go to the oven, bake it for five minutes. Now I brought it back. It was dry, and it was-- I don't have to worry that it is brittle. It will burn, right, [? and it will ?] be destroyed. And if I liked it very much, or whoever admired it very much, really insisted for a little more finish, she would let me go to my room, and I could even paint it. And I grew up with this, and it was always a very, very important thing for me, that what it did to me-- to my ego-- that I could create these things. And it was admired from early age. So when people ask me, do you have any background with your s-- sculpting? I say, well, bread. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked with br-- soft bread, and-- and that's the honest truth, that that's the way it started. But eventually, I realized that I was-- like the embroidery, and crocheting, and just lots of, lots of things-- whatever could be done by hand, I-- I just wanted to do it. I was good at it and did it all my life. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did things change in your community? When did you start experiencing anti-Semitic feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not in Sobrance-- not there, no, because that was-- um, how-- if I was 11 in [LAUGHS] 1922-- no, no, no. That came much, much later. We went into the city because, already, my two brothers were living there. And we paid-- so we found some nice family, and they accepted kids. Sometimes they were together, sometimes separately. And so they can go to-- for higher education, gymnasium, or high school, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I was 11, I too was sent to Uzhorod. The funny thing happened that, just before these times arrived, I met this couple from the city in our spa. And a girl-- their daughter, Martha-- and I kind of fell for each other, and we-- oh! Such a love affair. We shall never part. And we both made a resolution. The only way we shall go to the gymnasium, if we can live together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she knew that I have to go and stay someplace, because my parents still  lived in Sobrance. So it was arranged. I went, and I don't know how much they paid for my room and board. And oh, it was just real honeymoon. It was just fun, fun, crazy fun. And well, luckily, the lady was a good-natured lady and liked me, and I liked her. But we were two devils, and I didn't-- I can't believe it, how they could stand us. Anyway, she would crawl on the top-- the toilet was outdoors in Europe-- not in [NON-ENGLISH], actually. I'm surprised, now that I'm saying it. When we moved into the cit-- even in [INAUDIBLE], even in the small village of Sobrance-- I'm sorry-- our toilet was part of the house. But it seems that wasn't the standard, because when I moved into Uzhorod and stayed with this family, the-- I cannot call it bathroom. It was just strictly a-- um, uh, I wish I could think on it. It was th-- it was no bathtub, no nothing. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: An outhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just an outhouse, but I never saw it before, so I was kind of fascinated. It's outside, you know, and it's-- so what happened, one day we came home from school. And we did some drawings and everything, and Martha says, I like yours, and you like mine. Why don't we hang them up, and-- so we can enjoy and look at it? I said, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided, took it to the outhouse in her house, put it on the-- I shouldn't speak about such subjects. It's not so delightful. Anyway, she jumped on the top, and she almost fell all the way down. And that was no joke, because it was no-- there was no end to it. It was just tremendously deep and steep. But I remember for the rest of my life, when I say, outhouse, I just think of Martha, and me, and our paintings.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we did lots of fun things, and school was not really the right school for us. First of all, for me, it was difficult because they expected to-- that I speak now Czech, instead of Slovak. And it was very, very difficult. So anyway, after the year was over, I convinced my parents I want to go back to another school, a high school. And that's what I did, because Martha would go, and all my friends, and not the gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: The gymnasium requested to study, right away, Latin-- and very seriously-- and one other foreign language. It wasn't German. It wasn't French. I think Russian, Russian. So I said to my parents, I don't want it now. Why don't I just go to high school? And then I ended up with business school, a business academy for two years, and I graduated there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still learned lots of things there in the schools, which I don't think people are as well-prepared here, because from bookkeeping, to languages, to different s-- speed writing, to take shorthand writing-- I tell you, I was so well-prepared, I-- really. And the school was no problem. I always-- once I liked it, I-- I did real well. But the first year was very difficult, because, as I said, with the Latin and the Russian, it was just too much-- two extra new languages. But my parents moved in, themselves, to the city, by the time I was 13-- 12 or 13. And they bought a lovely home and lived-- we lived in-- lived in it-- lived in it for quite a while. Actually, all the-- we lived there all-- all our life, while we were in Czechoslovakia. And it was quite changed. Life was changed. It was much more strict there, much more supervised, different kind of [? peer-to-peer, ?] no more lice. And the friends-- one could pick them, and you didn't have to worry about those things. And as far of the division between the Jews and non-Jews, it was existing and everything, but no-- none of us felt as a disadvantaged person. It's true. My grandfather was very much respected in the city. And-- god, I hate to talk so much. It's like bragging. My-- I never saw my grandfather work in my-- in my lifetime, so which means from age 11, I ne-- so and before, for sure, he didn't work. So all I remember, he live-- had a beautiful, big desk. And he had a huge, beautiful pipe. And it had a ivory-- a little inlay finishing on it. And he would sit there and speak very seriously, very respectfully, that we had to behave ourselves. But it-- it-- it was-- it was a wonderful experience. It-- it was really-- there was-- there really-- we really knew our limitations. My two brothers didn't have it so easy. I think they-- they got some spanking from my father, who otherwise was such a easygoing, because they just didn't have that enthusiasm to excel in the school. They just managed, barely. So it wasn't too much of a joy for my parents. But generally speaking, the life in Uzhorod was wonderful because, as I said, we had relatives there. And we had nice exchange between cousins, and aunts, and uncles. It-- it was-- it was lovely to grow up.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].
